coredata.dc:title: An optimization strategy for photo-Fenton-like catalysts: Based on crystal plane engineering of BiVO4 and electron transfer properties of 0D CQDs
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Xueying
Liu, Yonggang
Zhai, Yunpu
Yu, Yonghao
Guo, Yaxin
Hao, Shiduo
coredata.dc:description: Herein, we report a novel Cu2(OH)3 F/CQDs-BiVO4 composite photo-Fenton-like system, which used BiVO4 and Cu2(OH)3F as electron donor and acceptor, respectively, and achieved efficient electron transfer between them through the electron bridging effect of Carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The material exhibited excellent ciprofloxacin (CIP) removal efficiency in the photo-Fenton-like coupled system. Cu2(OH)3 F/CQDs-BiVO4 had an incredibly fast response rate, eliminating 98.1% of CIP from the solution in just 1 h, according to the reaction kinetics. Exploratory tests proved that the catalyst kept up a sufficient level of activity across a wide pH range of 3–11 and in the presence of various anions. The activity, morphology, and crystal structure of the samples did not appreciably alter after five recycles. Finally, a possible reaction mechanism was also proposed based on the band structure, position and reaction species.

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Based on the above experiments, we reasonably speculated the intrinsic mechanism of CIP removal by CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO (Fig. 8). Since BiVO4 has a more negative conduction band position than Cu2(OH)3F, it causes spontaneous electron flow during the catalyst preparation, which increases the Cu(I) content in the Cu2(OH)3F lattice. These Cu(I) are converted to Cu(II) after rapid decomposition of H2O2 in a photo-Fenton-like environment, while strong oxidizing ·OH is generated as a way to achieve CIP degradation. CQDs, acting as both electron acceptors and donors, will capture the photogenerated electrons of BiVO4 and rapidly transmit them to the Cu2(OH)3F surface to superimpose with the internal electric field electrons of Cu2(OH)3F. These electrons with reduction potential will be used by Cu(II) produced by the Fenton-like process to regenerate Cu(I). Moreover, oxygen reduction will utilize a modest amount of electrons. The interleaved energy band structure of BiVO4 and Cu2(OH)3F allows the photogenerated electrons and holes to migrate to different bodies. To support the Fenton-like process, electrons are carried from BiVO4 to Cu2(OH)3F by CQDs, while in the opposite direction, holes are gathered in the valence band of BiVO4 to help remove CIP. With the assist of an effective electron transfer medium and the inherent electric field between the heterojunctions, a very high carrier separation efficiency is achieved, which considerably accelerates the removal of CIP.This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U21A20329), Key scientific research projects of higher education institutions in Henan Province, China (Project No. 21A150054) and International Cooperation Project of Henan Province, China (Project No. 172102410041).

This work was financially supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. U21A20329 ), Key scientific research projects of higher education institutions in Henan Province , China (Project No. 21A150054 ) and International Cooperation Project of Henan Province , China (Project No. 172102410041 ).

head.ce:title: An optimization strategy for photo-Fenton-like catalysts: Based on crystal plane engineering of BiVO4 and electron transfer properties of 0D CQDs

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, we report a novel Cu2(OH)3 F/CQDs-BiVO4 composite photo-Fenton-like system, which used BiVO4 and Cu2(OH)3F as electron donor and acceptor, respectively, and achieved efficient electron transfer between them through the electron bridging effect of Carbon quantum dots (CQDs). The material exhibited excellent ciprofloxacin (CIP) removal efficiency in the photo-Fenton-like coupled system. Cu2(OH)3 F/CQDs-BiVO4 had an incredibly fast response rate, eliminating 98.1% of CIP from the solution in just 1 h, according to the reaction kinetics. Exploratory tests proved that the catalyst kept up a sufficient level of activity across a wide pH range of 3–11 and in the presence of various anions. The activity, morphology, and crystal structure of the samples did not appreciably alter after five recycles. Finally, a possible reaction mechanism was also proposed based on the band structure, position and reaction species.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para:
As a coupled system of two advanced oxidation processes (AOP), photo-Fenton can deftly get around the drawbacks of photocatalysis and Fenton to effectively use electrons and boost the regeneration of low-valent metals (Deng et al., 2022; Shi et al., 2022). Due to the limitations of low pH and secondary contamination on the traditional homogeneous Fenton process based on soluble Fe(II), more and more studies are beginning to concentrate on non-homogeneous Fenton (Tian et al., 2021; Yang et al., 2022a). Copper stands out among them for its wide pH range and strong H2O2 decomposition ability (Lv et al., 2022). Recently, Fenton-like systems have used Cu-based catalysts, such as CuO, Cu2O, Cu-M bimetals, and layered double hydroxides (LDH) with copper participation (Li et al., 2022a; Teng et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2021a; Yang et al., 2022b). Unfortunately, difficulties with unstable structures and challenging production methods still exist for copper compounds. The exotic magnetic properties of a polyhydroxy copper halide material (Cu2(OH)3X, X = Cl, Br, I) with a polymorphic crystal structure caused by geometric frustration and complex phase transitions have been extensively investigated (Liu et al., 2017). And it is worth mentioning that Cu2(OH)3Cl coexisting with Cu(I) and Cu(II) performs well in the Fenton reaction (Wang et al., 2021b). Based on the properties of chemical bonds and bond energies, stronger Cu–F bonds will make the catalyst more stable when the halogen element is F with a smaller ionic radius. According to earlier work by our group, Cu2(OH)3F (CFO) can effectively decompose H2O2 in solution and exhibits good stability and durability (Wang et al., 2021c). More notably, Cu2(OH)3F can be produced under hydrothermal conditions at low temperatures. Therefore, this catalyst has a great advantage in terms of stability and efficiency as well as economics, and the generation of ·OH from H2O2 catalyzed by Cu2(OH)3F is mainly based on the following reactions (Zhao et al., 2021)：
(1) Cu(Ⅰ) + H2O2→Cu(Ⅱ) + OH− +·OH
(2) Cu(Ⅱ) + H2O2→Cu(Ⅰ)+ HO2·+ H+
Unfortunately, the slow process of reaction (2) inevitably causes a significant decline in the later reactions. Therefore, a strategy to accelerate the reduction process is urgently needed.

Numerous investigations have revealed that monoclinic BiVO4 (BVO) has excellent light utilization and electron transfer properties and is seen as a good electron donor for accelerating the regeneration of low-valent metals in Fenton-like processes (Zheng et al., 2022). The crystal structure of BiVO4 can be controlled by hydrothermal reaction conditions (pH, temperature and morphological directing agents) (Li et al., 2022b), and the selectively exposed crystal faces of BiVO4 reflect different active dominances. Under light irradiation, different surface energy band bending caused by different work functions can drive electrons and holes to the (010) and (110) facets, respectively. The (010) dominant BiVO4 has higher charge mobility and lower potential barrier for better photo-oxidation (Li et al., 2022c; Liu et al., 2021). Therefore increasing the exposure of the (010) facets will be more favorable for electron transport to the outside.

According to the explanation above, building Cu2(OH)3 F/BiVO4 heterojunctions might hasten the Cu(I) conversion. The Coulomb electrostatic force will draw the photogenerated electrons and holes and combine them to deactivate the catalyst if they are not used quickly enough. To accomplish quick electron transport and effective electron usage, more work is necessary. Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials with low cost and small particle size (<10 nm). They also possess special electrical characteristics as a result of the conjugated π system (Choi et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2022a). They are the perfect solid-state electron mediators because they can collect scattered electrons produced by metal oxides and can function as electron transfer routes between semiconductors for charge transfer and separation (Yao et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2021a). Additionally, it has been suggested that amino, hydroxyl, and carboxyl groups abundant in CQDs may participate in supramolecular self-assembly through hydrogen bonding, which has a moderating effect on the control of the microstructure, adjustment of the energy band structure, and improvement of the performance of the prepared photocatalysts. But the exact mechanism has not been reported (You et al., 2022).

Inspired by previous studies, we prepared CQDs and involved them in the crystallization process of BiVO4 to enhance the exposure of BiVO4 (010) crystalline surface (electron-rich surface) and synthesized regular decahedral BiVO4. Then, we successfully constructed a Cu2(OH)3 F/CQDs- BiVO4 heterojunction by growing CFO nanosheets in situ on the surface of CQDs- BiVO4. By using SEM, XRD, FT-IR, XPS, UV–Vis, and other techniques, the morphology, crystal structure, elemental information, chemical bonding information, and optical characteristics of the catalysts were tested. Ciprofloxacin (CIP), a refractory pollutant, was chosen as the indicative pollutant in an investigation on the catalytic activity and stability of Cu2(OH)3 F/CQDs-BiVO4 in solution. Finally, we combined the characterization analysis and radical capture results to elaborate on the electron transfer pathway and the mechanism of the photo-Fenton-like reaction.

All the reagents used in this experiment were analytically pure. Among them, bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O) and ammonium metavanadate (NH4VO3) were provided by Aladdin Reagent; nitric acid (HNO3) was provided by Haohua Chemical Reagent Co. Citric acid (C2H8N2) was purchased from Sinopharm, ethylenediamine (C2H6O) was provided by Kolon Chemicals Co., Ltd; hexamethylenetetramine (C6H12N4) was provided by Kaitong Chemical Reagent; sodium fluoride (NaF) was provided by Dean Reagent; copper nitrate trihydrate (Cu(NO3)2·3H2O), ammonia (NH3·H2O), and hydrogen peroxide (H2O2) were purchased from Windjammer Chemical Reagents. Deionized water was used in the experiments.
CQDs were prepared by a conventional hydrothermal-freeze-drying method, as described in Text.1 and Fig. S1.

As shown in Scheme 1 , NH4VO3 (6 mmol) and Bi(NO3)3·5H2O (6 mmol) were dissolved in 30 mL of nitric acid solution (2 M) and labeled as solutions A and B. Solution A was added dropwise to solution B to form a bright yellow solution, then CQDs of different masses were added. pH was adjusted to 2 by adding NH3·H2O dropwise after thorough stirring. A yellow precipitate was produced and aging was continued with stirring for 2 h. The product was then placed in a hydrothermal reactor and maintained at 200 °C for 24 h. The resulting product was washed and dried to give CQDs-BiVO4. The products with CQDs added at 1%, 3%, and 5% by mass are noted as CQDs (1)-BVO, CQDs (3)-BVO, and CQDs (5)-BVO, respectively.

1.44 g of Cu(NO3)2·3H2O and 0.84 g of C6H12N4 were dissolved in 50 mL of deionized water and 2 g of prepared CQDs (1)-BVO, CQDs (3)-BVO, CQDs (5)-BVO were added. pH was adjusted to neutral with NH3·H2O after thorough stirring. Then, 1.28 g of NaF was added, stirred for 30 min and kept in the reaction vessel at 105 °C for 3 h. The products were washed and dried and labeled as CFO/CQDs (1)-BVO, CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO, CFO/CQDs (5)-BVO.

X-ray diffraction (XRD) to determine the physical phase of the samples was achieved by a Nihon Rigaku Ultima IV X-ray diffractometer with Cu Kα as the radiation source ((λ = 1.5418 nm, 40 kV, 40 mA). The chemical bonding and functional group profiles in the samples were determined by Fourier to transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, Thermo Scientific Nicolet iS20). Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, Sigma 500, Resolution 0.8 nm, Carl Zeiss, United Kingdom) was used to observe the morphological structure of the samples. The electronic state of the sample surface was tested by an X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Thermo Scientific K-Alpha, United Kingdom). In addition, the absorbance properties were tested by UV–Vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV–Vis, Shimadzu UV-3600i Plus, the wavelength range: 200–800 nm). Photoelectrochemistry including transient photocurrent (i-t), electrochemical impedance (EIS), and Mott-Schottky plots (Mott-Schottky) were performed by an electrochemical workstation (CHI-660 E, Chenhua Instruments Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China; electrolyte solution: 0.1 mol/L Na2SO4; reference electrode: Ag/AgCl). The light source was a xenon lamp (300 W) equipped with a 400 nm cutoff filter.

The performance of the catalyst was evaluated by the effect of degradation ciprofloxacin (CIP) under certain conditions. The following experimental protocol was used for all the photo-Fenton-like experiments: 10 mg of catalyst was weighed and homogeneously dispersed in CIP solution (50 mL, 20 mg/L, pH = 7). The mixed solution was placed in a dark environment with continuous stirring for 30 min to reach adsorption-desorption equilibrium. Subsequently, 0.2 mL of H2O2 was injected dropwise to the mixed solution and placed under a 300 W xenon lamp (λ > 400 nm) for the reaction. About 3 mL of the mixture was removed at intervals and filtered through the filter head and injected into a quartz cuvette. Finally, the concentration of CIP was determined by UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The experimental protocol should be varied as needed when exploring the effect of catalyst, H2O2 concentration, and pH on the reaction.

The microstructures of all materials were observed using scanning electron microscopy (SEM). Cu2(OH)3F (Fig. 1 a) is a nanosheet structure with small particle size. Compared with the unmodulated irregular BiVO4 (Fig. S2), the BiVO4 initially modulated by ammonia (Fig. 1b) shifted its morphology to decahedral and the surface became smooth. BiVO4 displayed a full and regular decahedral structure with the insertion of CQDs in Fig. 1c. It could be as a result of CQDs' advantages in lowering the surface energy of the BiVO4 (010) surface (Sun et al., 2022). The CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO is shown in Fig. 1d, and it can be seen that the BiVO4 crystal's surface is covered with Cu2(OH)3F nanosheets, demonstrating the success of the heterogeneous structure's development.

TEM images of CQDs (3)-BVO are shown in Fig. 2 a and b. Black dots with particle size less than 5 nm can be clearly observed in Fig. 2b, which proves the successful introduction of CQDs. The surface loading between Cu2(OH)3F and BiVO4 can be observed from the TEM images of CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO (Fig. 2c and d). Fig. 2c does not show a clear morphology of CQDs due to the magnification limitation. However, the complete CQDs can be observed in Fig. 2d, and the lattice spacing of 0.226 nm, 0.241 nm and 0.253 nm can be measured, corresponding to the (101) surface of CQDs, (220) surface of Cu2(OH)3F and (002) surface of BiVO4, respectively (Wen et al., 2022), confirming the successful preparation of the ternary composites. EDS tests were performed on CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO to investigate the elemental distribution in the material. The results in Fig. 2e–j shows that the material contains Bi, V, Cu, F, O, and C elements, and each element is uniformly distributed.

Fig. 3 a shows the XRD patterns of all samples. The diffraction peak positions and intensities of the bare BiVO4 and Cu2(OH)3F are in high agreement with the standard cards JCPDS NO.14-0688 and JCPDS NO.16-0763 (Ou et al., 2018; Zhang et al., 2022b). The diffraction peaks are sharp and narrow with no impurity peaks, confirming that both the prepared Cu2(OH)3F and BiVO4 are single-phase and have high crystallinity. The lack of additional diffraction peaks in CQDs (3)-BVO compared to the original BiVO4 may be a result of CQDs' low crystallinity and little alteration, which have no impact on the crystal structure of BiVO4 (Li et al., 2022d; Wang et al., 2022a). It is worth mentioning that the diffraction peak at 30.46° of the modulated BiVO4 crystal is significantly enhanced compared to the irregular BiVO4 (Fig. S3), which is caused by the different (010) crystal plane exposure levels. While in CQDs (3)-BVO, the (010) crystalline plane diffraction peak is consistently enhanced, which shows that the introduction of CQDs is more favorable to the (010) crystalline plane exposure of BiVO4 crystals, which is consistent with the SEM results. This will accelerate the electron transfer during the reaction. The shaded area in Fig. 3b is magnified to highlight the Cu2(OH)3F diffraction peaks at 32.11°, 33.72°, and 36.84° of CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO, which demonstrate the existence of Cu2(OH)3F.

The FT-IR of Fig. 3c investigated the chemical bonding and functional group information of the samples. We assigned the peak at 739.81 cm−1 in CQDs (3)-BVO to the CC construction of CQDs (Fattahi Nafchi et al., 2022), whereas the absorption peak at 1051.71 cm−1 may be from C–O–C and C–O (Mandal et al., 2021). The integration of CQDs into BiVO4 was proven to be successful. We also examined the CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO spectrum. Because CQD loading was low, no pronounced absorption peaks were seen. The stretching vibration of Cu–F in Cu2(OH)3F appeared at 500.96 cm−1 and the absorption bands at 1637.76 cm−1,3448.43 cm−1 may be due to the crystal structure and the stretching of the adsorbed O–H vibrations and bending (Wang et al., 2021d). The successful compounding of the materials is further confirmed.

The chemical state of the elements in the catalyst was investigated by XPS. As expected, XPS scanning measurements of the spectra showed the presence of Bi, V, Cu, F, O, and C elements in the samples (Fig. S4). The fitted peaks at 159.24eV and 164.53eV in Fig. 4 a are induced by Bi(Ⅲ) and belong to Bi4f7/2 and Bi4f5/2, respectively (Li et al., 2020). It is noteworthy that in the complexes, the peaks are shifted towards higher energies, suggesting the electron loss of element Bi. V2p3/2,V2p1/2 in the energy spectrum of V2p (Fig. 4b) appear at 515.98eV and 523.48 eV, which is consistent with the +5 valence state V correlation description (Regmi et al., 2019). In the high-resolution XPS spectrum of Cu2p (Fig. 4c), the peaks representing Cu(I) and Cu(II) appear at 932.87eV, 935.31eV, respectively, generated by the spin-orbit splitting of Cu2p3/2 (Zhang et al., 2022c). By calculation, we found that the peak area ratio of Cu(I) to Cu(II) in bare CFO is 0.21:1, while the peak area ratio of Cu(I) to Cu(II) in CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO is 0.44:1. The Cu(I) content is significantly higher, and the fitted peaks of Cu2p orbitals are all shifted to the lower energy region. This is due to the increase of the surrounding electron cloud density caused by the absorption of electrons from BiVO4 by Cu2(OH)3F as an electron acceptor (Gao et al., 2022a). In Fig. 4d, F1s can be anti-folded into two peaks at 683.13 eV and 684.89 eV, probably as a result of the presence of Cu–F and O–Cu–F bonds (Wang et al., 2022b). The O1s deconvolution peaks (Fig. 4e) at 529.09eV, 530.93 eV are from the lattice oxygen and adsorbed oxygen of BiVO4, respectively, while the characteristic peaks at 531.74eV, 532.97 eV are attributed to Cu–O, O–H (Truong et al., 2022; Zhang et al., 2020). The three peaks at 284.97eV, 286.49eV, and 289eV in Fig. 4f correspond to non-oxidized cyclic C species (C–C/CC), C–O groups, and carboxyl C species in CO (Wang et al., 2020), demonstrating the presence of CQDs.

The UV–Vis spectrum of Fig. 5 a Shows the absorption of the sample. The absorption threshold of bare BiVO4 is about 520 nm, and Cu2(OH)3F exhibits a two-band absorption feature in the wavelength range below 400 nm and above 600 nm. The compounded CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO combines the absorbance characteristics of BiVO4 and Cu2(OH)3F and shows a wider light absorption range than the single phase, which also implies a higher light utilization. Besides, we calculated the band gaps of BiVO4 and Cu2(OH)3F as 2.40 eV and 2.46 eV, respectively by Tauc-Plot method.

To investigate the energy band situation of BiVO4 and Cu2(OH)3F, we tested their Mott-Schottky (Mott-Schottky) curves. Analysis of Fig. 5b shows that the flat-band potentials (EFB) of BiVO4 and Cu2(OH)3F are −0.71 eV and −0.52 eV (Ag/AgCl), corresponding to the standard potentials (NHE) of −0.51 eV and −0.32 eV, respectively. Also, the materials are all n-type semiconductors as can be determined from the positive slope of capacitance to voltage (Lin et al., 2021a), so the corresponding conduction band potentials of BiVO4 and Cu2(OH)3F should be −0.61eV and −0.42eV (NHE). Combining the above band gap calculations, the valence band positions of BiVO4 and Cu2(OH)3F can be determined to be 1.79 eV and 2.04 eV, respectively.

The electron transfer kinetics of the samples were further investigated by transient photocurrent (TPC) spectroscopy and electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS). From the (i-t) curve of Fig. 5c, it can be observed that CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO shows a higher photocurrent density than other photoanodes in several light-shade cycles, meaning the best charge separation efficiency (Lin et al., 2021b). The photocurrent response of CQDs (3)-BVO is stronger than that of bare BiVO4, which shows that the electron scavenging process of CQDs plays an important role in improving the carrier separation efficiency. By comparing the Nyquist curves of several photoanodes under bright and dark conditions (Fig. 5d), we found that the arc radius of CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO was significantly smaller than that of the CFO/BVO heterojunction, indicating that the internal CQDs could be used as electron transfer channels to reduce the resistance between Cu2(OH)3F and BiVO4. The above photochemical experimental results show that the combined structure between the electron transfer channel and the heterojunction successfully improves the electron transfer kinetics and electron separation efficiency.

Under the same circumstances, we conducted CIP deterioration studies with a number of samples. According to Fig. 6 a, after 1 h in the absence of the catalyst, CIP had only weakly autolyzed by around 6%. The decomposition activity increased after the participation of BiVO4, Cu2(OH)3F, and CFO/BVO, reaching 38.03%, 87.59%, and 91.71%, respectively. We successfully harvested the composite catalysts with various CQDs contents after adjusting the amount of CQDs throughout the preparation process in order to get the best electron transfer efficiency. All of the CIP removal rates after they were injected into the solution were more than 95%, with CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO exhibiting the best performance with a 98.1% removal rate. The pseudo primary rate constants of the catalysts modified with various CQD contents are higher than those of the CFO/BVO heterojunction and any single catalyst, according to the kinetic fitting results in Fig. 6d, and the removal rate of CIP by CQDs (3)-BVO is 2.95 times higher than that of bare BiVO4. In addition, it can be seen from Fig. S5 that the catalytic activity is much lower than that of the photo-Fenton coupling process, whether it is a single Fenton process or a single photocatalytic process. In summary, the heterojunction was greatly aided by the quick capture and transfer of photogenerated electrons through CQDs. This also improved the efficiency of charge separation and photoelectron utilization, and it permitted more electrons to participate in the decomposition of H2O2, which offered the catalyst a powerful CIP removal ability. However, the content of too low and too high CQDs hinders the photo-Fenton behavior, which may be related to the fact that CQDs affect the active sites and light absorption properties on the surface of BiVO4 (Xie et al., 2021).

We examined the performance of irregular BiVO4 and modified BiVO4 crystals through the degradation experiment of CIP and Rhodamine B(RhB) to confirm the impact of the (010) crystallographic surface on the BiVO4 crystals' ability to give off electrons (Fig. S6). The results suggest that in both the single phase and the heterojunction in combination with Cu2(OH)3F, the pH-modulated BiVO4 exhibits greater activity than the irregular BiVO4. This experiment confirms that the contribution of the crystal plane engineering of BiVO4 to the performance of CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO cannot be ignored. This is based on the effective electron gathering capabilities of the (010) mask. The electron acceptor will obtain additional electronic contact points as its exposure increases, enhancing the effectiveness of electron transfer and usage.

We investigated the reaction's response to catalyst dose and H2O2 concentration. As shown in Fig. 6b and e, the efficiency of CIP's degradation increased as catalyst dose increased, but when the dosage was increased to 0.6 g/L, it showed that the second half of the degradation curves tended to overlap with 0.4 g/L and the difference in reaction kinetic constants was marginal. As a result, we assumed that the dosage of 0.4 g/L was preferable from an economic standpoint. The performance of the catalyst will decrease due to the limits of active sites when too little catalyst is applied and the effects of light scattering and light shielding when too much is added (Zhang et al., 2021b). Furthermore, the degradation experiments with different doses of H2O2 concentration showed (Fig. 6c and f) that the catalytic efficiency was highest at the H2O2 concentration of 40 mmol/L, and the continued increase of the dosage was detrimental to the pollutant removal, which might be from the ·OH scavenging effect (H2O2 + ⋅OH→H2O + HO2⋅, HO2⋅ + ⋅OH→O2 + H2O), resulting in the quenching of ROS (Huang et al., 2020; Zhou et al., 2022).

A series of experiments were conducted to evaluate the adaptability and recoverability of the catalysts. It has been established that the system's acidity and basicity will affect the Fenton-like reaction in a significant way (Welter et al., 2022). We conducted studies for CIP removal by photo-Fenton using the catalysts in systems with pH values ranging from 3 to 11, respectively. The data in Fig. 7 a demonstrate that throughout a wide pH range, the efficacy of CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO on the elimination of CIP was maintained above 72%. However, a certain degree of reduction in catalytic effectiveness results from both too low and too high pH value. At low pH levels, H+ is present to cancel out the ·OH but at high pH levels, the substantial rise in OH− may cause Cu(II) to precipitate on the catalyst surface, restricting the reaction (Zhang et al., 2022d). The reaction is typically negatively impacted by the presence of several co-existing inorganic anions in wastewater treatment (Chen et al., 2022). To further examine the adaptability of the catalyst, humic acid (HA) and Cl−, CO3 −, SO4 2−, and NO3 − ions were introduced to the degrading system to resemble the real wastewater. The results in Fig. 7b show that the introduction of interfering ions only marginally reduced the removal efficiency of CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO, which was caused on by the scavenging action of different ions on ·OH (Liang et al., 2021).

We cleaned the used catalyst using deionized water and anhydrous ethanol, dried and recovered it, and then reused it to ascertain the recoverability of CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO. The elimination rate of CIP by CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO continued at nearly 90% after five cycles in Fig. 7c. XRD, and XPS were used to characterize the CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO after five recoveries. The XRD results in Fig. 7d demonstrate that repeated recycling had no effect on the physical structure of the samples. The XPS patterns in Fig. 7e further show that no new valence states for the copper species were produced in the samples before and after usage. In contrast, the Bi4f orbital in Fig. 7f exhibits the Bi(Ⅲ)/Bi(Ⅴ) coexistence state (Gao et al., 2022b). Similar to the Cu(I)/Cu(Ⅱ) transition, this phenomena is spurred on by the acquisition and loss of electrons during the photo-Fenton-like process. In light of the XRD data, it is obvious that this approach guarantees the photo-Fenton process' development without harming the catalyst's structure. The aforementioned results indicate that the photo-Fenton-like system based on CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO has excellent stability toward degradation of organic contaminants as well as considerable benefits in terms of resilience to pH fluctuation and water matrix tolerance.

We investigated the main active substances in the degradation of CIP by free radical burst experiments. To trap hydroxyl radicals (·OH), vacancies (h+), and superoxide radicals (·O2 −), respectively, p-benzoquinone (BQ), methanol (MeOH), and isopropyl alcohol (IPA) were used (Huang et al., 2022; Wang et al., 2022c). CIP decayed fast in the reaction system lacking scavengers, as depicted in Fig. S7. Other conditions were kept constant, and the application of various scavengers impeded the clearance of CIP to varying degrees. When IPA was used as the scavenger, the inhibitory effect was the strongest, demonstrating that the principal active ingredient ·OH was crucial in the elimination of CIP throughout the reaction. The next two were h+ and ·O2 −.

Based on the above experiments, we reasonably speculated the intrinsic mechanism of CIP removal by CFO/CQDs (3)-BVO (Fig. 8 ). Since BiVO4 has a more negative conduction band position than Cu2(OH)3F, it causes spontaneous electron flow during the catalyst preparation, which increases the Cu(I) content in the Cu2(OH)3F lattice. These Cu(I) are converted to Cu(II) after rapid decomposition of H2O2 in a photo-Fenton-like environment, while strong oxidizing ·OH is generated as a way to achieve CIP degradation. CQDs, acting as both electron acceptors and donors, will capture the photogenerated electrons of BiVO4 and rapidly transmit them to the Cu2(OH)3F surface to superimpose with the internal electric field electrons of Cu2(OH)3F. These electrons with reduction potential will be used by Cu(II) produced by the Fenton-like process to regenerate Cu(I). Moreover, oxygen reduction will utilize a modest amount of electrons. The interleaved energy band structure of BiVO4 and Cu2(OH)3F allows the photogenerated electrons and holes to migrate to different bodies. To support the Fenton-like process, electrons are carried from BiVO4 to Cu2(OH)3F by CQDs, while in the opposite direction, holes are gathered in the valence band of BiVO4 to help remove CIP. With the assist of an effective electron transfer medium and the inherent electric field between the heterojunctions, a very high carrier separation efficiency is achieved, which considerably accelerates the removal of CIP.

In this work, the electron transfer medium is cleverly introduced based on crystal plane engineering and heterojunction. CFO/CQDs-BVO was successfully prepared and applied in the photo-Fenton-like coupling system. We have verified the beneficial impacts of CQDs on BiVO4 morphological modulation and crystal plane exposure level using a variety of characterization techniques. The high (010) facet exposure of BiVO4 shows a stronger electron-giving ability. More notably, relying on the ability of CQDs to shuttle electrons, the efficient transfer and utilization of photogenerated electrons was achieved, which not only blocked the recombination path of carriers in the photooxidation procedure but also realized the regeneration of Cu(I) in the Fenton-like procedure, resulting in more active species (ROS) production. This strategy breaks the limitations of both photocatalysis and Fenton-like and provides an effective way to optimize the coupled system of advanced oxidation technology.

Yunpu Zhai: Writing-Review & Editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Yonghao Yu: Validation, Resources, Investigation, Writing-Review & Editing. Yaxin Guo: Validation, Investigation, Writing-Review & Editing. Shiduo Hao: Validation, Investigation, Writing-Review & Editing.
